raw_case:
Ovarian vein syndrome – Rare ovarian vein compression of the ureter
Ovarian vein syndrome is a rare condition involving the compression of the ureter by the ovarian vein. Since it was first described, very few cases have been reported in literature. We present a case of a 37-year-old female with typical symptoms and common right-side involvement. The imaging findings on ultrasound, CT, and nuclear medicine imaging are classic for her condition.1

Introduction

Ovarian vein syndrome (OVS) is a rare condition caused by external compression of the ureter by a dilated ovarian vein resulting in ipsilateral hydroureter and hydronephrosis. OVS is characterized by nonspecific findings such as abdominal/lower back pain, recurrent urinary tract infections (UTIs), and urinary symptoms such as renal colic, hematuria, dysuria, urinary frequency, and urinary urgency.1,2 Ovarian vein syndrome was first described by Clark in 1964,1 but relatively few cases have been reported in literature since. Thus, the exact prevalence of OVS is unknown.2

Case presentation

A 37-year-old female presented with macroscopic hematuria and dysuria. Urinalysis and urine culture showed no signs of a UTI. Initial ultrasound imaging of the abdomen and pelvis (Fig. 1) found mild hydronephrosis in the right kidney with good flow in the distal ureter on color doppler imaging. No structural abnormalities were identified on ultrasound to explain the patient's macroscopic hematuria.Follow-up imaging was done using multiphasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis (Fig. 2). CT again found mild hydronephrosis in the right kidney. The ureteropelvic junction was dilated with compression from a dilated right ovarian vein crossing distally measuring 9 mm in diameter. There was no delayed nephrogram or cortical thinning to suggest any functional significance to this compression.To assess functional impairment, diuretic scintigraphic renography (Fig. 3) was performed using Technetium (Tc)-99m mercaptoacetyltriglycine (MAG3) and furosemide administered 10 minutes prior to imaging. The renal scan showed normal and relatively symmetric renal perfusion. The split function measured 44 % on the left and 56 % on the right. The kidneys demonstrated normal bilateral emptying.Finally, cystoscopy was performed to confirm the absence of an obstructing lesion. Given a normal renal perfusion scan and adequate investigation to rule out an obstructing lesion, there was no indication for intervention at the time.3

Discussion

The pathophysiology of OVS is thought to be multifactorial resulting from a combination of ovarian vein dilation, connective tissue fixation, and hormonal changes. Ovarian vein dilation can be secondary to variant embryological development such as persistent posterior subcardinal veins and is also seen in pregnancy secondary to external compression by a gravid uterus.1,2 It is noted that while the ovarian veins may be dilated during pregnancy, there is a relatively small decrease in venous pressure.1,2 Thus, compression of the ureter is likely facilitated by additional connective tissue fixation of the ovarian vein to the ureter.1,2 Fluctuations in estrogen and progesterone levels in pregnancy are also thought to contribute to ureteric compression due to a decrease in tone of the ureteric wall.1,2 Progesterone increases the flow in the ovarian vein while estrogen causes relaxation of the smooth muscle in the ureter.OVS is most commonly right-sided and often seen in multiparous women. The right ovarian vein drains directly into the inferior vena cava, so aberrant veins such as persistent embryologic vasculature are more likely to drain into the right ovarian vein resulting in dilation.1 While in pregnancy, there are physiologic changes that result in dilation of the ovarian vein and increased ureteric compressibility.The initial imaging modality for OVS is ultrasound. Diagnostic features on ultrasound include a dilated ovarian vein ≥6 mm, hydroureter at the level of L3/4, and possible reversal of ovarian vein flow on color doppler.1 When ultrasound is inconclusive, CT or MRI is the next step with diagnostic clues including dilation of the ovarian vein ≥8 mm and ipsilateral hydroureter.1Treatment can be explored in cases of significant symptoms or functional impairment. Medical treatment of ovarian vein syndrome include medoxyprogesterone acetate or goserelin acetate which cause venous contraction.1 If refractory to medical management, interventional radiologic therapies such as coil embolization and sclerotherapy can be used to embolize the ovarian vein 1. Finally, surgical ovarian vein ligation is another effective option. Several case reports of surgical treatment of OVS can be seen in literature.3,4,5,64

Conclusion

Cases of ovarian vein syndrome are uncommonly presented in literature. Patients often present with non-specific findings of pain and urinary symptoms. We present a case of right ovarian vein syndrome with typical symptomatology and imaging findings.

Patient consent declaration

Patient consent was not required as the patient's identity was no compromised in this case report.

Funding source declaration

The authors have no funding sources to declare.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Ryan K.W. Chee: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Reshma M. Koshy: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Mitchell P. Wilson: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Gavin Low: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

Declaration of competing inerest

The authors have no conflicts of interest to declare.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 37-year-old female  
- **Chief Complaint:** Macroscopic hematuria and dysuria  
- **History of Present Illness:** Non-specific; patient presented with urinary symptoms.  
- **Past Medical History:** None provided.  
- **Personal History:** None provided.  
- **Family History:** None provided.  
- **Physical Examination:** None provided.  
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - Urinalysis/urine culture: No signs of a UTI.  
  - Ultrasound: Mild hydronephrosis in the right kidney, no structural abnormalities.  
  - CT Scan: Mild hydronephrosis in the right kidney; dilated right ovarian vein (9 mm diameter) compressing the ureteropelvic junction. No delayed nephrogram or cortical thinning noted.  
  - Diuretic scintigraphic renography: Normal, symmetric renal perfusion (left 44%, right 56%); kidneys showed normal bilateral emptying.  
  - Cystoscopy: Normal, no obstructing lesions.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Urinary Tract Infection (UTI):** Excluded as urinalysis and urine culture revealed no signs of infection.  
2. **Kidney Stones or Other Ureteral Obstructions:** Excluded through imaging (ultrasound, CT, cystoscopy) that showed no obstructive lesions or structural abnormalities.
3. **Other Causes of Hydronephrosis (e.g., tumor, congenital anomalies, external compression):** Focus transitioned to an external compression cause after imaging identified a dilated right ovarian vein compressing the ureter. 
4. **Ovarian Vein Syndrome (OVS):** Considered based on the combination of macroscopic hematuria, dysuria, mild hydronephrosis, and imaging findings of a dilated ovarian vein with ureteral compression. The diagnosis was supported by the absence of significant functional impairment as evidenced by normal renal perfusion and kidney emptying on scintigraphy.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The final diagnosis is **Ovarian Vein Syndrome (OVS)** caused by external compression of the right ureter by a dilated ovarian vein. Key diagnostic features include the imaging findings of mild hydronephrosis and a dilated ovarian vein crossing the ureter without functional significance on nuclear imaging.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 37-year-old female with macroscopic hematuria and dysuria. No UTI based on urinalysis and culture. So first, UTI is ruled out. Next, imaging shows mild hydronephrosis on the right. Ultrasound and CT confirm this. The CT also mentions a dilated right ovarian vein compressing the ureteropelvic junction (UPJ). Hmm, that's interesting.

Hydronephrosis without obstruction on diuretic renography? The scintigraphy showed normal perfusion and emptying. So maybe the hydronephrosis isn't causing significant functional obstruction. But the CT shows the ovarian vein is compressing the UPJ. What's that called? Oh right, ovarian vein syndrome, which is a type of pelvic congestion syndrome. It can cause ureteral compression, leading to hydronephrosis. But why would that cause hematuria and dysuria?

Wait, the dilated ovarian vein pressing on the ureter might cause intermittent obstruction or irritation. The hematuria could be from venous congestion or maybe varices around the ureter or bladder. Also, dysuria without infection could be due to the irritation from the compression. 

Cystoscopy was normal, so no bladder lesions, tumors, stones. So the source of bleeding isn't from the bladder. Maybe upper tract? But imaging didn't show stones. The hydronephrosis is mild and only on the right. So the right ovarian vein dilation compressing UPJ might be the culprit here.

Possible diagnosis: Right ovarian vein syndrome causing ureteropelvic junction obstruction leading to hydronephrosis and subsequent hematuria and dysuria. Another term for this is Pelvic Congestion Syndrome with ureteral compression. Alternatively, May-Thurner-like syndrome but for the ovarian vein. Since there's no obstruction on renography, maybe the compression is intermittent or positional. 

Other differentials: Nutcracker syndrome (left renal vein compression), but this is on the right. Maybe a ureteral stricture, but CT didn't show that. Endometriosis? Possible, but no mention of cyclical symptoms. 

The key here is the dilated ovarian vein compressing the UPJ. So the final diagnosis would be right ovarian vein syndrome causing extrinsic ureteral compression leading to hydronephrosis and associated hematuria/dysuria.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with macroscopic hematuria and dysuria. Urinalysis/culture rules out UTI, eliminating common infectious causes.  
<step 2> Imaging identifies mild right hydronephrosis. Ultrasound and CT confirm no structural abnormalities but highlight a dilated right ovarian vein (9 mm) compressing the ureteropelvic junction (UPJ), suggesting mechanical obstruction.  
<step 3> Diuretic renography shows normal renal perfusion and emptying, indicating no functional obstruction. This suggests intermittent or positional compression rather than persistent blockage.  
<step 4> Cystoscopy excludes bladder pathology, narrowing the source of hematuria to the upper urinary tract or vascular/compressive etiology.  
<step 5> The dilated ovarian vein compressing the UPJ aligns with ovarian vein syndrome, a variant of pelvic congestion syndrome. Venous congestion or mechanical irritation from the engorged vein likely explains hematuria and dysuria.  
<step 6> Other differentials (e.g., nephrolithiasis, stricture, malignancy) are excluded by imaging and cystoscopy. Nutcracker syndrome is unlikely due to the right-sided localization.  

### Answer:
Right ovarian vein syndrome with extrinsic ureteropelvic junction compression causing hydronephrosis and hematuria.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with macroscopic hematuria and dysuria. Urinalysis/culture rules out UTI, eliminating common infectious causes.

Imaging identifies mild right hydronephrosis. Ultrasound and CT confirm no structural abnormalities but highlight a dilated right ovarian vein (9 mm) compressing the ureteropelvic junction (UPJ), suggesting mechanical obstruction.
Diuretic renography shows normal renal perfusion and emptying, indicating no functional obstruction. This suggests intermittent or positional compression rather than persistent blockage.
Cystoscopy excludes bladder pathology, narrowing the source of hematuria to the upper urinary tract or vascular/compressive etiology.

The dilated ovarian vein compressing the UPJ aligns with ovarian vein syndrome, a variant of pelvic congestion syndrome. Venous congestion or mechanical irritation from the engorged vein likely explains hematuria and dysuria.
Other differentials (e.g., nephrolithiasis, stricture, malignancy) are excluded by imaging and cystoscopy. Nutcracker syndrome is unlikely due to the right-sided localization.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) excluded as urinalysis and urine culture revealed no signs of infection.
Kidney stones or other ureteral obstructions excluded through imaging (ultrasound, CT, cystoscopy) showing no obstructive lesions or structural abnormalities.
Focus transitioned to an external compression cause after imaging identified a dilated right ovarian vein compressing the ureter.
Ovarian Vein Syndrome (OVS) considered based on macroscopic hematuria, dysuria, mild hydronephrosis, and imaging findings of a dilated ovarian vein with ureteral compression.
Diagnosis supported by normal renal perfusion and kidney emptying on scintigraphy.

Final diagnosis: Ovarian Vein Syndrome (OVS) caused by external compression of the right ureter by a dilated ovarian vein. Key diagnostic features include mild hydronephrosis and a dilated ovarian vein crossing the ureter without functional significance on nuclear imaging.
